companyCaseStudy.title: How Twitter Scaled the Home Timeline: Hybrid Fan‑Out, Caches, and a Million‑Follower Problem
companyCaseStudy.summary: Twitter serves hundreds of millions of timelines every day and historically saw spikes of tweets-per-second in the hundreds of thousands during breaking events. To avoid a write storm from a single celebrity with millions of followers, Twitter moved to a hybrid fan‑out model, heavy caching, and careful trimming — slashing work, storage, and latency while keeping timelines fresh.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook — the million‑follower problem

At scale, one user's action can explode into millions of downstream writes. Historically Twitter handled roughly 500M tweets/day (≈6k tweets/sec average) with large peaks during breaking events. A single celebrity post with 10M followers could mean 10M writes if you naively push every tweet into every follower inbox.

That kind of write amplification destroys throughput, increases storage, and makes migrations and rate limits painful. The problem: how do you keep timeline reads fast (<100ms), keep write costs manageable, and ensure the experience remains near‑real‑time for most users?

---

## Problem → Challenge

- Problem: Deliver a personalized, reverse chronological (and increasingly ranked) home timeline to hundreds of millions of users in near‑real time.
- Challenges:
  - Write amplification: fan‑out‑on‑write from prolific accounts can cause millions of writes per event.
  - Tail latency: assembling timelines on read can add tens or hundreds of ms if we aggregate many sources live.
  - Storage & IO: materializing full inboxes for all users multiplies storage needs and increases replication/backup costs.
  - Operational complexity: migrating or rebalancing huge per‑user inbox tables is risky at scale.

## The Big Idea: Hybrid Fan‑Out + Caching + Trimming

Twitter's practical solution: don't choose fan‑out‑on‑write or fan‑out‑on‑read wholesale. Use a hybrid model:

- Fan‑out‑on‑write for the common case (normal users and modest follower counts): when a normal user tweets, the system pushes the tweet into each follower's precomputed inbox. This makes reads cheap for the majority of users.
- Fan‑out‑on‑read for the pathological case (high‑follower producers): for celebrities and very high‑fanout producers, the system *does not* push to every follower. Instead, it stores the producer's recent posts in a compact producer store. When a follower reads, the timeline service merges the follower's precomputed inbox with recent posts from heavy producers on the fly.
- Strong caching (Manhattan / memcache / Redis): read paths use a multi‑tier cache (per‑user timeline cache + global caches) to reduce assembly work.
- Inbox trimming & quotas: precomputed inboxes are kept to a bounded size (e.g., O(800) items) and older items are trimmed. This bounds storage and I/O per user.
- Thresholds & heuristics: define follower‑count and activity thresholds to decide whether to push or pull for a given producer.

### Architecture (simplified)

```mermaid
graph LR
  User[Client] -->|timeline request| Edge[Edge Service / API]
  Edge --> TimelineSvc[Timeline Service]
  TimelineSvc --> Cache[(Per-user Timeline Cache)]
  TimelineSvc --> InboxDB[(Precomputed Inbox Store)]
  TimelineSvc --> ProducerStore[(Heavy-producer Store)]
  TimelineSvc --> RankingSvc[Ranking / Reorderer]
  RankingSvc --> Edge
  InboxDB -->|writes| FanoutWorkers[Fanout Workers]
  FanoutWorkers --> InboxDB
  ProducerStore -->|writes| ProducerWrites[Producer Write Path]
```

- FanoutWorkers are partitioned by user‑id shards to distribute the heavy write work evenly.
- ProducerStore keeps a compact recent buffer per heavy producer (for example, last N tweets), which is cheap to fetch and merge.

## Why the hybrid model works (numbers & results)

- Write reduction: for producers above the fanout threshold, the system avoids pushing to millions of followers. If one celebrity has 10M followers, switching that producer to fan‑out‑on‑read saves ~10M writes per celebrity tweet. Multiply by thousands of celebrity tweets and you save billions of writes/day.
- Lower storage: bounding per‑user inbox size reduces per‑user storage and makes replication/backup predictable.
- Better tail latency: most reads hit the precomputed cache, giving low median latency (tens of ms). Rare merged reads for heavy producers still fit within acceptable SLOs because the producer buffer is small and cached.

### Before / After (illustrative)

| Metric | Before (push-everything) | After (hybrid + cache + trim) |
|--------|--------------------------:|-------------------------------:|
| Avg writes/day (system-wide) | 10s of billions | reduced by 20–60% depending on load (billions fewer) |
| Storage for inboxes | Unbounded growth proportional to followers | Bounded per user (e.g., 800 items) |
| Median timeline latency | 100–200ms | 30–80ms |
| Tail latency (p99) | 500–1000ms (during spikes) | 150–400ms |

> Note: the percentages above are representative of observed operational improvements reported in public engineering posts and conference talks. Exact savings vary by traffic shape and thresholds chosen.

## Implementation details & operational tricks

- Threshold selection: pick follower‑count thresholds (e.g., 5k, 50k, 500k) and tune based on cost models. Producers above a threshold are switched to fan‑out‑on‑read.
- Per‑producer buffer sizes: keep only the most recent N items for heavy producers (N typically 50–500) so the read merge is cheap.
- Partitioning fanout workers by follower‑id range (consistent hashing) to avoid hotspots and make rebalancing incremental.
- Inbox trimming policy: time + count-based eviction (e.g., keep up to 800 items or up to 7 days of tweets, whichever smaller).
- Backpressure & queueing: fanout writes are queued in durable queues (Kafka), workers consume at sustainable rates and expose backpressure if the system can't keep up.
- Monitoring & SLOs: track metrics like write‑per‑tweet, inbox growth rate, time to fanout completion, cache hit ratio, and timeline p50/p95/p99 to detect regressions.
- Migration path: flip thresholds gradually (canary by geography or % of users) and monitor the change in writes and latency.

## Real operational wins

- Dramatic write reduction during spikes: by treating a small percentage of producers differently, the system avoids large write storms that used to cause queue pileups and degraded tail latency.
- Predictable storage & replication costs: trimming inboxes made replication windows and backup durations predictable and much faster.
- Simpler incidents: with hybrid design, incidents involving fanout workers affect a smaller portion of traffic; fallback (merge on read) keeps the product usable even if precomputation lags.

## Drawbacks & tradeoffs

- Increased read complexity: timeline reads now sometimes require on‑the‑fly merges (costly if not cached), so read path SLOs must be watched carefully.
- More moving parts: you must operate two codepaths (push & pull) and the heuristics determining which to use.
- Ranking & freshness: mixing precomputed items and live pulls complicates ranking and deduplication — additional logic is required to avoid showing duplicates or reordering incorrectly.

---

## TL;DR (the story arc)

Problem: fanout on write causes write storms from high‑follower producers.

Challenge: keep reads fast and consistent while preventing write explosion.

Solution: hybrid fan‑out (push most, pull the extreme cases), strong caching, inbox trimming, and thresholds — and partitioned fanout workers for scalable writes.

Result: billions of writes avoided, predictable storage, and timeline latency reduced to the tens of ms for most users while staying within acceptable p99 bounds during spikes.
keyTakeaways: Use a hybrid fan‑out strategy: push for the common case, pull for the extreme cases to avoid catastrophic write amplification.
Bound per‑user state (inbox trimming) to keep storage and replication predictable — e.g., keep an ordered cap like 500–1000 items.
Partition fanout work and use durable queues + caching; instrument write/read metrics and flip thresholds gradually while monitoring SLOs.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
def assemble_timeline(user_id, limit=100):
    # 1) Try fast path: per-user cached timeline
    cached = cache.get(f"timeline:{user_id}")
    if cached and len(cached) >= limit:
        return cached[:limit]

    # 2) Read precomputed inbox (bounded, fast)
    inbox_items = inbox_db.fetch(user_id, limit=limit)

    # 3) Read recent items from heavy producers the user follows
    heavy_producers = follow_graph.heavy_producers_followed_by(user_id)
    live_items = []
    for p in heavy_producers:
        live_items.extend(producer_store.get_recent(p))

    # 4) Merge, dedupe, rank, and trim
    merged = merge_dedupe_time_sorted(inbox_items, live_items)
    ranked = ranking_service.score_and_rank(merged)
    result = ranked[:limit]

    # 5) Populate cache for subsequent reads
    cache.set(f"timeline:{user_id}", result, ttl=5)
    return result

concept.title: Ship Compartments for Your Services: The Bulkhead Pattern Explained
concept.summary: When one component misbehaves it shouldn't sink the whole system. The *bulkhead pattern* isolates capacity (threads, connections, memory) so failures are contained, enabling graceful degradation and better overall availability.
keyTakeaways: Bulkheads isolate capacity (threads, connections, memory) so a failing dependency can’t exhaust system-wide resources.
Use semaphores, separate pools, or container-level isolation combined with timeouts and circuit breakers for best effect.
Instrument and tune: wrong sizing, retries, or missing timeouts can undermine the pattern’s benefits.
